name: Dimensional (DIM) Weight, imperial divisor
description: Dimensional weight in pounds: total shipment volume in cubic inches divided by a carrier-published divisor in in3 per lb (e.g. 139 for FedEx and UPS Daily Rates). US parcel carriers bill light-but-bulky packages on this figure instead of the scale weight.

inputs.name: Pieces
inputs.description: Number of identical packages; use 1 for a single parcel.

inputs.name: Divisor
inputs.description: Carrier-published DIM divisor; must be greater than zero. Common imperial values: 139 (FedEx; UPS Daily Rates), 166 (UPS Retail Rates; USPS until 2026-07-11).

outputs.name: Dimensional weight
outputs.description: DIM weight the carrier compares against actual weight when rating.

assumptions: Dimensions are entered in inches and the divisor is in in3 per lb, so the result is pounds.
All packages share the same outer dimensions.
No carrier rounding is applied inside the formula.
limitations: Carriers typically round each dimension (nearest or next whole inch, by their own rule) before computing, and round the DIM result up to the next whole pound — both can push the billed figure above this raw result.

Divisors vary by carrier, service, rate card, account agreement, and date — e.g. FedEx publishes 139 in3/lb, UPS 139 for Daily Rates and 166 for Retail Rates, and USPS moves from 166 to 139 on 2026-07-12. Confirm the current value in your carrier's service guide.
The metric variant of this formula uses centimetres and a divisor in cm3/kg (see freight.volumetric_weight.divisor); do not mix unit systems within one calculation.
Some carriers apply dimensional weight only above a size threshold (e.g. USPS applies DIM pricing above 1,728 in3).

examples.note: One 30 x 24 x 22 in package at divisor 139: 15,840 in3 / 139 = 113.96 lb (a carrier would round this up to 114 lb).

sources: FedEx 'What is dimensional weight?' page (fedex.com/en-us/shipping/packaging/what-is-dimensional-weight.html, checked 2026-07-02): L x W x H in inches / 139 for US/Puerto Rico/international packages.

UPS 'Shipping Dimensions and Weight' support page (ups.com/us/en/support/shipping-support/shipping-dimensions-weight, checked 2026-07-02): divisor 139 for Daily Rates, 166 for Retail Rates; DIM result rounded up to the next whole pound.
